bp_title: Implement least privilege access and permissions boundaries for agentic workflows
question_stem: GENSEC05 — How do you avoid excessive agency for models? (verified 2026-06-08 from gensec05.html). Excessive agency is an OWASP Top 10 LLM threat, typically introduced through agentic architectures: an agent takes actions beyond its intended purpose.
verbatim_text: GENSEC05-BP01 — Implement least privilege access and permissions boundaries for agentic workflows. Implementation guidance: agents automate processes or call external functions using FM reasoning; excessive agency = an agent decides the best solution is to take broader actions beyond its scope (not malicious, an unintended consequence of automation; the agent has little knowledge beyond the prompt of what is permitted). Develop permission boundaries on FM requests and agentic workflows: the role making a model request should only access the systems, guardrails, and data sources needed for the response; same for agentic workflows (Bedrock agents have execution roles, Flows have service roles), least-privilege especially for roles facilitating access to data sources (Kendra) or compute (Lambda); permission boundaries + least privilege should be shared across models, especially where multiple models/agents service a prompt. Create developer roles specific to tasks (separate IAM roles for the prompt engineer vs the security engineer creating the agent's IAM service role) = separation of duties. Define permission boundaries (the maximum permissions a role can be given), implementable at account level. Define permission boundaries and policy documents in the org's AI policy document with instructions to modify as workload requirements change. Implementation steps: (1) Review org IAM guidelines to determine the best path to create least-privileged roles (SCPs for central control over max permissions; reuse org templates/policies). (2) When creating IAM roles for agents, define a scoped policy with least privilege access (specify intended resource ARNs for permissions + API actions; consider conditions to restrict to trusted traffic e.g. specific VPC). (3) Attach the policy document to a role assumable by a specific set of agents (permission boundaries at role level to prevent inadvertent authorization; condition statements in the role's trust policy). (4) Implement user confirmation for the agent, requiring users to confirm agent actions and mitigating the risk of excessive agency. Resources cross-ref SEC02-BP01/02/06, SEC03-BP01/02; OWASP Top 10 for LLMs; Bedrock User Confirmation; Verified Permissions + Bedrock Agents.
outcome_note: GENSEC05 walk started 2026-06-08. Single BP (BP01). Steps 1-3 are generic IAM least-privilege / permission-boundary discipline (same base-WAF cross-refs as GENSEC01-BP01, which was not_promoted). Step 4 (user confirmation for agent actions) + the OWASP excessive-agency framing is the GenAI/agentic-distinct slice — agent ACTION authorization, unowned by any sibling (GS1B3-01 read/retrieval, GS2B1-01 output, GS4B2-01 input; none govern agent tool execution / writes). Candidate under discussion: a per-agent tool/action allow-list + confirmation-gating for high-impact actions, the ACTION-side member of the guardrail family. HALT for user discussion before promote/not_promote.
open_items: Rule out legacy PRIN_008 (Agent Security Framework). Decide: promote (action-control principle) vs not_promoted (generic IAM like GENSEC01-BP01). Focus-area placement if promoted (new P24 — Agentic Action Control?).
next_candidates: GENSEC06 (Data poisoning); GENOPS02 / GENOPS05; GENCOST03-BP02; GENREL.
